channel.title: +0 on Vishvas's notes
channel.description: Recent content in +0 on Vishvas's notes

item.title: Acknowledgments
item.description: The authors of this book first gathered in the spring of 1992, at the Graduate School of the City University of New York, to embark on a discussion of the “Italian Southern Question, Representations and Realities.” Shaped by several different disciplines - anthropology, history, literature, sociology, political science - and by scholarly traditions on both sides of the Atlantic, they discovered, first and foremost, that they shared a great deal of common ground regarding this problem.

item.title: Contents
item.description: Acknowledgments Introduction: The Dynamics of Neo-orientalism in Italy (1848–1995) *Jane Schneider * Part I The Genesis of the Question 1 Before the Southern Question: “Native” Ideas on Backwardness and Remedies in the Kingdom of Two Sicilies, 1815–1849 *Marta Petrusewicz * 2 The Emergence of the Southern Question in Villari, Franchetti, and Sonnino *Nelson Moe * 3 How Many Italies? Representing the South in Official Statistics *Silvana Patriarca * 4 Biology or Environment?

item.title: Index
item.description: agency, capacity for historical action, 68-9, 87, 123, 129-30, 137, 140, 150, 211-2, 240, 285 particular problem for peasantry, 14-5, 145-8, 166, 186-7, 282-3 “passive revolution,” concept of, 31, 153 n.11, 205, 212 see also collective action, fatalism, peasantry agonism, “culture of mistrust,” 1, 6-7, 12—3, 20, 211, 229-31, 258, 283 as theme in Sicilian literature, 120-2, 126-7, 258, 281 as impediment to social action, 14, 18, 256 Agrarian Question, see land tenure system, land reform

item.title: Introduction: The Dynamics of Neo-orientalism in Italy \(1848–1995\)
item.description: *Jane Schneider * In Italy, and in Italian studies, the “Southern Question” evokes a powerful image of the provinces south of Rome as different from the rest of the peninsula, above all for their historic poverty and economic underdevelopment, their engagement in a clientelistic style of politics, and their cultural support for patriarchal gender relations and for various manifestations of organized crime. This tenacious catalogue of stereotypes includes, as well, the notion that southerners, by dint of their very essence, or at least their age-old culture and traditions, possess character traits that are opposite to the traits of northerners.

item.title: Notes on Contributors
item.description: Michael Blim is Associate Professor of Anthropology in the PhD Program in Anthropology at the Graduate School of the City University of New York. He is the author of Made in Italy: Small-Scale Industrialization and its Consequences (1990) and the co-editor with Frances Rothstein of Anthropology and the Global Factory: Studies of the New Industrialization in the Late Twentieth Century (1992). Other works include articles for the Einaudi Storia d’Italia, Politics and Society, Critique of Anthropology, and Identities.
